sports_feed._note: Refreshed by the refresh-sports-feed scheduled task. Each item is {headline, url, source, published (ISO8601), snark}. Snark voice: Barstool-adjacent guy-at-the-bar, crass, horny, cocky, zero filler. One sentence. No slurs, no punching down at specific real people in a harmful way. Punch sideways (at media, front offices, rival fanbases) and up (at ownership). Keep it Boston.

items.headline: Red Sox vs Braves tonight: 18-24 Sox try to keep bats hot in Atlanta

ranked_opportunities.role: Senior Director, Subscription & Data Solutions @ LevelTen Energy | Techstars Job Board
ranked_opportunities.verdict: This role requires deep domain experience in the renewable energy PPA market that Paul does not possess. The JD explicitly requires "10+ years of experience in the renewable energy market, with deep knowledge of PPAs, energy procurement, and the buyer/seller/advisor/developer ecosystem" and "Significant commercial or sales experience within this market, you have personally sold to or managed relationships with energy buyers, developers, utilities, or advisors." Paul's 15 years of commercial infrastructure experience is in satellite monitoring (oil & gas, fishing surveillance, maritime), not renewable energy transactions. While the role structure, commercial-product bridge, subscription packaging, data monetization strategy, maps beautifully to Paul's skills from GFW (building commercial licensing for an open platform, multi-stakeholder deal architecture), the sector mismatch is a hard blocker. RF capped at 5 because the responsibilities fit Paul's profile but the required domain does not. IL capped at 4 because Paul cannot credibly claim the named renewable energy market experience or customer base. The role is well-compensated and the stage is interesting (post-PMF data platform scaling subscriptions), but Paul would need to explain a full sector pivot from space/maritime to renewable energy, and the JD signals they want someone who has "been living and breathing this market for years."

blockers.type: Hard requirement: 10+ years renewable energy market experience
blockers.detail: JD requires '10+ years of experience in the renewable energy market, with deep knowledge of PPAs, energy procurement, and the buyer/seller/advisor/developer ecosystem.' Paul has 15 years in commercial space/satellite sector (KSAT 2011-2021, GFW 2021-2026) but not in renewable energy PPAs, energy procurement, or the utility/developer ecosystem that LevelTen serves. His infrastructure work was water/wastewater utility security (PA Consulting) and satellite monitoring, not renewable energy transactions.

blockers.type: Hard requirement: Significant commercial/sales experience selling to energy buyers, developers, utilities, advisors
blockers.detail: JD requires 'Significant commercial or sales experience within this market, you have personally sold to or managed relationships with energy buyers, developers, utilities, or advisors and understand how deals get done.' Paul's sales experience is in satellite monitoring services to oil & gas, fishing surveillance, and maritime clients. He has not sold to renewable energy buyers, PPAs, or clean energy developers.

ranked_opportunities.verdict: This is a carbon credit sales role at an Asia-focused carbon project platform. While the mission aligns strongly with Paul's climate/sustainability focus and the entrepreneurial framing is appealing, the core work is enterprise carbon credit sales ,  managing pipeline, closing deals, forecasting ,  not building a commercial function or creating demand in a new market. The JD explicitly requires "7-10 years of experience in enterprise sales, business development, strategic account management" and describes the role as "responsible for the entire carbon credits sales cycle." Paul's KSAT and GFW experience demonstrates commercial development and multi-stakeholder deal architecture, but he is not a quota-carrying enterprise seller. RF capped at 5 because the role shape does not match Paul's market-builder identity. IL at 5 because Paul has relevant carbon/climate sector knowledge (GFW commercial licensing, KSAT EO for environmental compliance) but lacks the named "carbon market sales experience strongly preferred." SA scores 6 because Rubicon is described as expanding and the role is "newly added due to business expansion," but the role itself is about selling an existing product (carbon credits) to enterprises, not building the commercial function. SPM scores 7 because multi-stakeholder carbon credit deals and navigating complex corporate buyers fits Paul's pattern, even though the role itself is misaligned. Singapore preference is a soft geographic blocker.

blockers.detail: Role has 'strong preference for candidates based in Singapore.' Paul is based in Taos, NM. While location is described as 'flexible,' the preference signal creates friction.
Role is 'entire carbon credits sales cycle' including 'managing pipeline, executing account strategies' ,  this is quota-carrying enterprise sales, not market-building.

ranked_opportunities.verdict: This role fails on three hard constraints. First, geographic: Singapore-based with strong preference for local candidates; Paul is in Taos and Singapore relocation is a substantial ask. Second, the JD explicitly requires "10, 15+ years of relevant client-facing deal making experience with 5-7 years+ in carbon markets, climate finance, project investing" ,  Paul has 5 years total in the climate/carbon space (GFW 2021-2026), not 5-7+ in carbon markets specifically. Third, the role demands "deep expertise in carbon markets, including project pathways, quality guardrails, offtake structures, supply and demand dynamics" and "significant experience originating, structuring, negotiating, and executing complex multi-million dollar commercial transactions, with a focus on environmental commodity transactions." Paul built a commercial licensing program at GFW; he did not invest in carbon projects, structure offtakes, or negotiate carbon credit purchase agreements. RF capped at 5 (role involves deal-making and multi-stakeholder coordination but in a domain Paul lacks depth), IL capped at 4 (experience gap is explicit and quantified), GC capped at 2 (Singapore residency preference is explicit). SPM scores 8 because the multi-stakeholder deal architecture, portfolio construction, and market-building patterns are strongly present, but that does not overcome the hard blockers.

blockers.detail: Role based in Singapore with strong preference for Singapore-based candidates. Paul is based in Taos, NM and while open to relocation, Singapore represents a significant geographic and cultural distance.

ranked_opportunities.verdict: This role is enterprise carbon credit sales in Asia, a market Paul has never sold in, from a geography where he is not based. The **hard requirement cap fires on RF and IL**: the JD states "At least 7-10 years of experience in enterprise sales, business development, strategic account management, or commercial origination, preferably involving complex solutions for large corporate customers" and "Carbon market sales experience strongly preferred." Paul meets the enterprise sales structure and complexity, but he has zero carbon market sales experience and zero Asia commercial network. The "strong preference for candidates based in Singapore" is not a residency requirement, but it signals a strong soft blocker. **RF = 5**: Paul has deep consultative enterprise sales experience (KSAT, GFW), C-level engagement, and complex solution sales, but this is carbon credits, a market he has never sold in. **IL = 4**: Paul's network is EO/maritime/IUU fishing, not carbon or Asia enterprise buyers. **SA = 6**: Rubicon is described as "fast-evolving," "newly added due to business expansion," but it is NOT a greenfield commercial function, it's scaling an existing carbon sales engine. **SPM = 7**: Multi-stakeholder coordination, consultative selling to corporates with sustainability mandates, and building markets in emerging regulatory environments all pattern-match Paul's career, but this is sales execution in an existing market, not market creation. **GC = 3**: Singapore preference is a major friction point. Total weighted = 50.5, rounded to 51. This is pursue_with_caveats: the strategic patterns fit, but the domain and geography gaps are material.

blockers.detail: Role strongly prefers Singapore-based candidates. Paul is US-based in Taos, NM. While the JD says 'location is flexible,' the strong preference for Singapore creates friction for a US-based candidate.

ranked_opportunities.role: Account Executive @ Gable | Techstars Job Board
ranked_opportunities.verdict: This is a traditional quota-carrying Account Executive role with territory assignment and quarterly sales targets. The JD explicitly states responsibilities: "Own and exceed quarterly and annual sales targets within an assigned territory," "Hunt for new logos," "Collaborate with SDRs to develop territory strategies," and "Maintain accurate pipeline management, forecasting, and activity tracking in HubSpot." Paul's profile explicitly states he is a "market builder, NOT market inheritor" and "NOT a quota-carrying hunter." There is no evidence in the JD of the regulatory precedent work, multi-stakeholder deal architecture, or demand creation that characterizes Paul's career patterns. The role is about executing a sales process in an established category (workplace/flex office management software) to established buyer personas, not building the market itself. This is a clear mismatch on role fit and candidacy, despite the company potentially being at an interesting stage.

ranked_opportunities.role: Senior Customer Success Engineer @ Rasa | Techstars Job Board
ranked_opportunities.verdict: This role is a hard pass. RF is capped at 3 because the JD explicitly requires "deep technical expertise in Rasa or equivalent conversational AI or NLP/LLM-based technologies" and hands-on experience with "Python or Full Stack apps that run in the cloud, ideally DevOps experience with Kubernetes." Paul's technical expertise is in satellite/EO systems (SAR, AIS, vessel detection), not AI/ML product implementation. IL is capped at 2 because Paul has no demonstrated conversational AI, NLP, or LLM development background, and the role explicitly seeks someone who has been "part of a key customer leadership team" in this domain. The role itself is technical enablement and reactive support for existing enterprise customers ("reactive issue resolution," "technical guidance," "optimize their AI assistants"), not commercial demand creation or market building, Paul's core identity. Even if Paul could learn Rasa, the job is not aligned with his market-builder profile.

watch_list.why: Montreal-based methane and CO2 detection from space; 15-satellite operational constellation; launched two new satellites at COP30 in Belém November 2025. Customers span every supermajor and most regulators (Shell, ExxonMobil, BP, Chevron, Pemex, EDF, Carbon Mapper Coalition). Dual-customer model ,  sell the same data to operators and to regulators monitoring those operators ,  is direct KSAT lineage. Active hiring across analytics, science, BD; English-speaking Canadian HQ with EU presence. ENI-era methane work translates one-to-one.
watch_list.roast: GHGSat is still the methane detection company everyone knows and nobody quite understands how to buy from, which is a branding problem masquerading as a market education problem. They have satellites, they have aircraft, they have ground sensors, they have a SaaS platform, and they have a data catalog that every oil major and half the environmental regulators on earth have looked at without quite pulling the trigger. You have a warm relationship there, which is the best kind of relationship to have with a company that might be hiring but is not posting roles. The domain fit is perfect. You sold oil and gas monitoring at KSAT when methane was still just a line item in an environmental permit, and you sold data partnerships at GFW when the buyers did not quite understand why they needed a commercial relationship. GHGSat has both of those problems, plus the added complexity of a product portfolio that spans hardware, data, and analytics, which means the sales cycle is long, the deal structure is bespoke, and the buyer is never quite sure whether they are buying a satellite tasking contract, a data subscription, or a consulting engagement. If GHGSat ever opens a senior commercial role, you should be the first call they make. If they do not, you should make the call yourself, because the market is moving, the regulatory pressure is building, and the companies that figure out how to turn methane detection from a science project into a commercial category are going to own the next decade. You know how to do that. The question is whether GHGSat knows they need you.

watch_list.why: San Francisco / Bangalore climate forecasting platform serving ag, food, and finance. Series B $22M (Four Rivers Group, Radical Ventures, Yaletown, Neotribe) brought total to $38M. Customers include Dole, Driscoll's, Nuveen Natural Capital, Advanta Seeds. ARR grew 5x in the 18 months post-Series A. Commercial team scaling ,  direct fit for Paul's enterprise data licensing muscle (KSAT to ENI to GFW pattern). Bangalore presence means a Norwegian Air-friendly time zone overlap from Taos.
watch_list.roast: San Francisco climate forecasting outfit that sells Dole six-month-out predictions for what avocado prices will do when Mexican drought breaks the next heat record, and somehow has Driscoll's, Nuveen Natural Capital, and Advanta Seeds on the customer list paying real money for it. The pitch lives in the gap between weather forecasting (boring, commoditized, free) and climate scenarios (academic, wrong, useless to a procurement officer staring at a Q3 budget) ,  sub-seasonal probabilistic intelligence with measurable margin impact. They closed Series B around $22M led by Four Rivers Group with Radical Ventures and Yaletown along for the ride, bringing total raised to $38M, which is enough to fund a real commercial team and exactly the stage where the wrong VP of Sales hire torpedoes a company that could have been a category-defining win. ARR went up 5x in eighteen months post-Series A, which means the product works and the buyer cycle is shorter than the typical 'climate intelligence' Slack-thread death-march. The customer list looks like your KSAT enterprise rolodex with the cuisine swapped: ag majors instead of energy majors, food companies instead of fishing fleets, and procurement officers who all read the same Bloomberg piece on how climate volatility is going to eat their margins. Your KSAT enterprise muscle and the GFW commercialization scars translate cleanly: convince a Fortune 500 buyer that satellite-fueled forecasting is worth $250K/year while their CFO's first instinct is 'isn't this what AccuWeather does for free.' Bangalore presence means a Norwegian Air-friendly time zone overlap from Taos. Send a 'hello' before some Stanford MBA who's never sold a B2B subscription before convinces the CEO they can run commercial.

watch_list.why: Bremen-based, first satellite launching 2026, VHR optical + AI analytics, European defense angle fits KSAT/GFW pattern perfectly
watch_list.roast: German very-high-resolution EO startup that just closed an oversubscribed 5.3 million euro seed round in December 2025 and is launching their first satellite in Q4 2026. Multispectral, visible through shortwave infrared, sub-meter resolution, built in Bremen, which is the least sexy place in Europe to build satellites but possibly the most competent. Marble has an ESA Copernicus Contributing Missions contract worth 3 million euros, anchor customers already signed, and a plan to get to 20 satellites by end of 2028. They offer terrain analytics, trafficability assessment, coastal monitoring, and object detection, which is the same feature set you spent three years at KSAT trying to convince ConocoPhillips they needed. The company is dual-use, meaning they sell to defense and climate customers without having a personality disorder about it. You know this market. You know the buyers. You know the operational cadence required to go from five satellites to twenty without the wheels coming off. The founders are technical, the investors are European, and the growth curve is real. First satellite launches in six months. If you were there now, you would be writing the go-to-market playbook for satellite two. Instead, you are here, watching them do it without you, wondering if the Bremen winter is worse than Tromso.

watch_list.why: Bangalore, first multi-sensor satellite (SAR + optical on one platform) launching Q1 2026, 160kg, targeting insurance/defense/ag
watch_list.roast: Indian startup launching the first satellite that puts SAR and optical on the same bus, which sounds like the kind of technical elegance that makes an engineer happy and a commercial buyer confused. You spent three years at GFW trying to explain why someone would pay for vessel detection data that a free platform already shows. GalaxEye has the opposite problem. They have a genuinely differentiated technical product and now they need to find the buyer who understands why multi-sensor fusion matters before the competitor launches a simpler single-sensor product at half the cost. The market is insurance, defense, ag, infrastructure, which is startup-speak for we will sell to whoever answers the phone. You know this game. The technical capability is never the constraint. The constraint is finding the three verticals where multi-sensor data creates a decision advantage worth the contract price, then building the partnerships that make the buyer trust you before the data exists. Bangalore puts you twelve hours ahead of Taos, which is either a feature or a disaster depending on how you feel about morning calls with the UK and evening calls with California. The company has traction, the tech is legitimately differentiated, and they are at the stage where the commercial function needs to be built with intention instead of hope. The question is whether you want to build the India EO market from scratch, or whether you would rather do the same work for a company that already has European or US distribution. If the answer is the former, this is the role. If the answer is the latter, keep looking.

watch_list.why: Berlin-based, ESA-backed, building Twinspector constellation for infrastructure monitoring, secured seven-figure ESA funding April 2026
watch_list.roast: German company that convinced ESA to write a seven-figure check to build a dedicated constellation for infrastructure monitoring, which means somebody in Europe finally decided that proactive utility monitoring is a business instead of a science project. You know the pattern. KSAT sold Shell on proactive offshore monitoring by proving the regulator would eventually require it. LiveEO is running the same play but for power lines and pipelines on land instead of rigs at sea. The difference is the utility sector moves slower than oil majors, which is saying something. They have traction, they have ESA backing, and they just closed funding in April 2026, which means they are probably hiring for the next phase right now. The commercial challenge is identical to what you faced at KSAT in 2013. The technical capability is proven. The buyer knows they need it eventually. The question is how do you make eventually become now. You do that by finding the utility that has the most exposure, running a paid pilot that creates a precedent, then using that precedent to move the regulator. Once the regulator moves, every other utility has to buy or risk looking negligent. You have run this play twice. You could run it again for LiveEO, but this time for European utilities instead of Australian offshore oil. The role would be building the commercial function and the regulatory strategy in parallel, which is exactly the kind of market-making work you do well. The downside is Berlin is not Taos, and the utility sector is slower than oil and gas, which you may have finally lost patience for. But if the idea of getting paid to move a regulator appeals to you, the bones are right.

watch_list.why: Israeli startup using satellite data, climate models, and AI to build resilience platforms for land-based supply chains (agriculture, forestry). Direct EO application, climate-adjacent, mission-commercial tension Paul knows well.
watch_list.roast: Marvin is an AI copilot for resilient agricultural supply chains, which sounds like someone fed a venture pitch generator the words satellite, climate, and value chain and hit enter three times. The company is Israeli, the domain is hard, and the customer is an agribusiness trying to figure out whether a vegetation index means replant or wait two weeks. You spent a decade at KSAT convincing oil majors that proactive monitoring beats reactive crisis management. Marvin is selling the same argument to a different vertical, except the regulator is slower, the margin is thinner, and the buyer does not know what threshold triggers action. They raised money, they have a product, and someone will eventually figure out what the copilot is supposed to do when the model says the harvest fails in June. The opportunity is proving that the platform can sell to more than one crop, more than one country, and more than one skeptical procurement officer who has seen five satellite startups pitch this year. If you can translate KSAT's multi-vertical playbook into agricultural risk management, this is the kind of builder-stage infrastructure company you could actually help scale. If not, it is a really nice slide deck about the future of food systems.

watch_list.why: Converts biomass into carbon-rich liquid, injects it underground for permanent removal. Durability-focused, scaling commercially, direct climate impact with verifiable MRV. Paul's GFW commercial-mission framing applies cleanly.
watch_list.roast: Charm Industrial takes agricultural waste, turns it into bio-oil, and pumps it underground where it stays for thousands of years. It is one of the few carbon removal pathways that does not require you to pretend enhanced weathering is ready for prime time or that direct air capture pencils out without a hyperscaler writing the check. They have actual removal credits sold, actual injection sites operating, and actual customers who care whether the carbon stays buried or leaks back out in fifteen years. You built the Planet deal at GFW by knowing which buyers pay for verified outcomes instead of aspirational offsets. Charm is the same bet in a different market. The carbon credit world is a wasteland of consultants, registries, and startups that cannot explain their methodology without a whiteboard and twenty minutes. Charm is one of the few that can say we put it in the ground, here is the well, here is the monitoring, here is the third party attestation. The commercial challenge is not the science. It is whether enterprises actually allocate budget to durable removal or whether they keep buying cheap forestry credits and calling it net zero. If Paul can help them frame the value proposition so the CFO and the chief sustainability officer both nod, they win. If not, it is another breakthrough technology waiting for the market to catch up.

watch_list.why: Building satellite and radar-based extreme weather forecasting systems for agriculture, logistics, energy. High-frequency EO + AI, enterprise customers, operational decision-making use case. Adjacent to Paul's GFW real-time data experience.
watch_list.roast: Climavision is trying to make weather forecasting fast enough and accurate enough that someone will actually change their logistics route, delay a harvest, or shut down a transmission line before the storm hits instead of after. The company runs its own weather radars, ingests satellite data, wraps it in AI-driven prediction models, and sells subscriptions to industries that lose money every time they guess wrong about a thunderstorm. The pitch is solid. The technical execution is real. The business model is recurring revenue from enterprises that already budget for weather intelligence. The problem is the same problem every high-frequency EO company eventually hits. Buyers believe the data matters right up until they have to integrate it into an actual operational workflow, at which point someone in the organization says we already have NOAA for free and this costs how much per seat. You sold KSAT oil spill monitoring into a market that had no category and no budget line. Climavision is selling into a market that has both but assumes the free version is good enough. If Paul can help them articulate why sub-hour forecasting is worth enterprise budget and how to structure a pilot that proves ROI in the first 90 days, they have a shot. If not, it is another weather company with better models and no path to the decision maker who actually signs.

watch_list.why: Dutch EO startup using passive microwave sensing for soil moisture, vegetation, and weather insights. Agriculture, insurance, banking, commodities verticals. Established European player, commercially mature buyer base. Paul's GFW insurance work (AXA Climate, British Marine/QBE) is directly adjacent, and the multi-sector licensing model echoes KSAT's multi-vertical services.
watch_list.roast: VanderSat sells microwave-derived soil moisture and vegetation data to agriculture, insurance, banking, and commodities buyers, which is a polite way of saying they sell the same dataset to four different industries and call it four different products depending on who is asking. They have been at this since 2014, so they are not a moonshot, they are a functioning commercial operation with actual recurring customers, which in the EO world makes them a statistical anomaly. The microwave angle is clever because it works through cloud cover, which means they can monitor crops in the Netherlands in February, which is otherwise impossible and also depressing. The challenge is that they are in the most competitive segment of EO. Every agtech platform, every precision agriculture startup, and half the insurance industry has a satellite data partnership. VanderSat has survived a decade, so they clearly have something defensible, but Paul would need to understand what it is. Is it the microwave sensor advantage? Is it the algorithm? Or is it just that they were early and the customers have no reason to switch? If it is the third one, that is fine, but it means the growth story is about adding new verticals, not expanding within existing ones. Paul has done that. GFW's insurance vertical was net-new, built from a use case the buyers did not know they had. If VanderSat wants to find the fifth vertical, he has run that play.

watch_list.why: UK startup building climate risk reporting tools using satellite EO data for biodiversity, drought, heat stress, wildfire. Targets businesses, legislators, environmentalists. Aligns with evolving environmental laws. Paul's GFW conservation mission, open-access commercial tension, and Planet partnership (monitoring use case creation) are all relevant. UK-based, small team, mission-commercial balance.
watch_list.roast: Map Impact sells climate risk reporting tools built on satellite data, which sounds like every ESG analytics pitch from the last four years except they actually name the tools BiodiversityView, DroughtView, HeatView, WildfireView, like they are releasing a Marvel franchise. They are a UK startup, probably six people in a co-working space in Shoreditch, and they are trying to turn satellite-derived risk scores into something a compliance officer will pay for annually. The opportunity is real. EUDR is in force, CSRD is rolling out, and every corporation with a supply chain in a forest is suddenly very interested in whether that forest still exists. The risk is that Map Impact is one layer removed from the actual pain. They do not sell to the companies that own the land, they sell to the companies that report on the companies that own the land. That is a viable business, but it is a services business dressed up as a data business, and the margins reflect it. Paul has spent his whole career one step closer to the operational buyer, the person who actually has to make a decision based on the data. If Map Impact wants to move upstream and sell directly to the asset owner or the supply chain manager, that is a different motion, and Paul has done it. GFW built the ASN subsea cable deal by identifying a non-obvious use case for vessel detection data and then constructing the commercial argument from scratch. Map Impact has the data and the regulatory tailwind. The question is whether they can build the argument that makes the buyer feel like they have no choice but to pay.

watch_list.why: Satellite-based soil carbon MRV at 90% cost reduction. Profitable while competitors burn cash. 2M+ tonnes annually. Earthshot Prize winner. Global South focus.
watch_list.roast: You spent a decade at KSAT selling oil spill monitoring to super majors who could afford your day rate and you walked Planet's 1.2 billion hectare negotiation through three parties who each had their own commercial truth to protect. Boomitra is what happens when someone finally decides the entire voluntary carbon market has been designed backwards. They use satellites and AI to measure soil carbon for smallholder farmers in India, Argentina, Paraguay, places where the per-farm payment is measured in hundreds of dollars, not hundreds of thousands, and where traditional MRV would cost more than the carbon credit is worth. They claim 90% cost reduction. They registered South America's first grassland restoration project. They are profitable, which in the soil carbon space is like saying you found the Ark of the Covenant in a storage unit in Fresno. Most of their competitors are still trying to convince enterprises in Denver that regenerative ag is a line item, and they are out there issuing credits to people who have never heard the term additionality and do not care. The Technology is not the problem anymore, the problem is whether anyone actually believes the market will pay for soil carbon at scale, and Boomitra has a model that works whether the VCM fixes itself or not. They raised $4M, Yara Growth Ventures led it, and they secured $35M in project financing to cover five million acres across nine countries. That is not a pilot, that is a program. You have spent your entire career figuring out how to sell satellite data to the buyers no one thought would pay for it, how to structure deals when the obvious customer is not the right customer, and how to build commercial programs in spaces where the use case has to be explained before the product can be sold. Boomitra has done that in the most complicated corner of the carbon market and the question is whether you want to be part of the next phase or whether you want to watch someone else build the commercial licensing model you would have designed. They are in Menlo Park, which is not Taos, but they are fully remote, which is.

watch_list.why: Regenerative ag, agroforestry, biochar, enhanced weathering. Sells through Puro, Isometric, Verra, Gold Standard. $20M Series B first tranche Feb 2026, WestBridge Capital led, targeting $45M full round.
watch_list.roast: Varaha is what happens when someone finally admits that no single carbon removal pathway is going to save the voluntary carbon market and decides to build a portfolio instead of a religion. They do regenerative agriculture, agroforestry, biochar, enhanced rock weathering. They sell credits through Puro, Isometric, Verra, Gold Standard, and Carbon Standards International, which means they have figured out how to navigate five different verification regimes without setting their hair on fire, a skill you learned the hard way when you were trying to get KSAT into EMSA and AMSA at the same time and every regulator had their own version of what oil spill monitoring was supposed to mean. They raised $20M in their Series B first tranche in February, WestBridge Capital led it, they are targeting $45M for the full round, and they are based in Gurugram, India, which is a twelve-hour flight from Taos but a direct match for the complexity you have spent twenty years managing. The carbon markets right now feel like the EO sector in 2011, everyone knows something is about to happen, no one knows which business model will survive, and the companies that win are going to be the ones who can talk to buyers in five different languages and sell the same ton of carbon three different ways depending on who is signing the check. You built GFW's commercial licensing program by figuring out which derived products had private sector value on top of an open-access platform, and Varaha is doing the same thing but in reverse, they are figuring out which removal pathways have market traction and then building the MRV and verification stack to support whatever the buyer wants to hear. They are not remote, they are India-based, but they are scaling fast and they need someone who understands how to build the commercial function when the product is new, the market is skeptical, and the regulatory environment is a moving target. That person is you, or it is someone who learned everything you know by making your mistakes five years from now.

watch_list.why: Amsterdam-based. Combines satellite monitoring, field sensors, mobile apps. Tracks carbon, biodiversity, water benefits, and socioeconomic outcomes. Holistic MRV for agroforestry and reforestation.
watch_list.roast: Treecycle is an Amsterdam-based MRV platform for agroforestry and reforestation projects, and if you are wondering whether the world needs another forestry carbon MRV startup the answer is probably not, but if you are wondering whether the world needs one that actually tracks socioeconomic metrics alongside carbon sequestration the answer is yes and that is what Treecycle does. They combine satellite monitoring, field sensors, and mobile applications that local project managers actually use, which is a harder problem than it sounds because most MRV systems are designed by people in Berlin who have never tried to get a field manager in Tanzania to upload data on a phone with intermittent signal. They track carbon, biodiversity, water benefits, and community economic outcomes, which is the kind of holistic monitoring that makes NGOs weep with joy and makes corporate buyers nervous because it is harder to reduce to a single number on a slide. You spent three years building the Planet Labs deal for GFW, and the reason that deal worked is because you understood that the use case was not just small vessel detection, it was also proving that GFW could process 200 million hectares a day and still deliver outputs that passed scientific review, and Treecycle is doing the same thing but in the agroforestry space, they are proving that MRV can be comprehensive and still be affordable. They are European, which means they are dealing with a regulatory environment that actually cares about biodiversity co-benefits and does not treat carbon as the only metric that matters, and they are building a platform that works for projects that are too small for the Veras of the world to care about but too important to ignore. You have built commercial programs in spaces where the customer does not know they need the product yet and the product has to be explained before it can be sold. Treecycle is in that space right now. Amsterdam is not Taos, but it is closer than Tromso, and the work is the kind of thing you would design if someone gave you a blank sheet of paper and said build the MRV platform the carbon markets should have had ten years ago.

watch_list.why: Leading independent verifier for nature-based carbon, Amazon/Airbnb/Netflix customers, Gates + Lowercarbon backed
watch_list.roast: Pachama uses satellite imagery and machine learning to verify forest carbon projects. They do not plant the trees. They do not own the land. They just tell you whether the carbon everyone else is claiming is actually there. That makes them the referees in a game where half the players are accused of cheating and the other half are trying to prove they are not. Their customer list is Amazon, Airbnb, Netflix, and Nespresso. Their investor list is Bill Gates' BEV, Chris Sacca's Lowercarbon, and Amazon Climate Pledge. That is the kind of backing that makes them the default verification vendor for anyone who does not want to get burned buying forest offsets. The market only works if the credits are real, and Pachama has become the leading independent answer to that question. Paul's entire KSAT career was built on selling the commercial argument that proactive monitoring creates value even when no one wants to admit they need it yet. Pachama is doing the same thing but in carbon markets instead of oil spills. The difference is the voluntary carbon market is a mess of competing standards, questionable baselines, and buyers who got burned in 2023 when half the credits they bought turned out to be hot air. Pachama is the MDA of forest carbon except they are platform-agnostic and their competitive moat is trust, not hardware. Paul has negotiated with Planet, Airbus, MDA, and Maxar. He understands how imagery providers price into enterprise deals and how verification businesses build margin on top of someone else's data. Pachama is fully remote, mission-driven, and backed by people who will fund patient growth. The job would be building the commercial function for the company that everyone in carbon has to use but no one wants to admit they are paying for. That is Paul's entire professional biography in one sentence.

watch_list.why: Multi-sensor platform play, AWS integration, climate and agriculture verticals map directly to GFW and KSAT value-add portfolio
watch_list.roast: Bangalore outfit with a $36 million Series B and hyperspectral birds that see what your PlanetScope misses. They closed that round in March 2024 and you are just now hearing about them, which tells you everything about how fast this market is moving and how slowly you check your feeds. Six bands is table stakes now. Pixxel is flying 150-plus and calling it actionable environmental intelligence, which is consultant speak for we can tell a healthy soybean from a stressed one at 5 meters. They have AWS Ground Station stitched in for the enterprise crowd and a pitch deck aimed at agriculture, mining, and carbon monitoring. The founders came out of IIT and they did the hard thing, which is build the sensor themselves instead of licensing someone else's. You spent three years at KSAT selling oil spill detection to the same twelve buyers. Pixxel is productizing the whole vertical stack and signing the contracts you wish GFW had been allowed to chase. If you can stand one more Zoom call with a founder under 30 who uses the word game-changing without irony, this is the one.

watch_list.why: Left-field adjacency: if satellite data licensing taught you anything, it is that the real money lives where no one thinks to look until someone builds it. Resource extraction in LEO is five years out or twenty depending on who you ask. Early mover advantage matters.
watch_list.roast: Los Angeles space mining company that rides Rocket Lab to orbit and wants to refine platinum off asteroids before anyone else figures out the export paperwork. You are thinking this is not climate tech and you would be half right. It is extraction infrastructure for a world where we stop digging up the Earth because we can dig up everything else instead. They raised a seed round, launched two test missions, and have not yet brought back a single gram of anything valuable, which makes them the purest form of venture bet you can find. The thesis is sound if you squint. The execution window is decade-long. The commercial argument is we sell to battery manufacturers and semiconductor fabs who need rare earth metals and do not want to depend on one country that happens to control 70 percent of supply. You sold oil spill monitoring to ExxonMobil when no one believed the business case. Astroforge is selling metal from space to no one yet. But the team is serious and Rocket Lab keeps launching their stuff, which means someone believes. You would too, if you were still 32 and living in Tromso with nothing to lose but time.

watch_list.why: Direct GFW adjacency. RF-based vessel detection, dark ship tracking, IUU fishing applications. Built the market you were trying to create at KSAT 2016-2018. Luxembourg-based, ESA partnership, ASX-listed. Mature enough to have revenue, small enough to need someone who knows how to sell it.
watch_list.roast: Luxembourg company with an ESA stamp and a handful of RF satellites that can geolocate a ship transmitting VHF in the middle of the Pacific when its AIS is off. You spent five years at GFFW explaining to people why this matters and now Kleos has operationalized it and taken it public on the ASX, which is the Australian exchange where mining companies go to die and space companies go to get liquidity before anyone in Silicon Valley notices them. They have a constellation called Patrol, they have customers in defense and maritime risk, and they have the one problem every RF geolocation company has, which is the commercial market does not quite understand what they are buying yet. You do. You sold this exact concept to SkyTruth in 2012 before the market existed. You closed the Shell MLA in 2015 by explaining proactive monitoring when MDA had the incumbent contract. Kleos has the tech and the heritage and the operational constellation. What they need is someone who can walk into a room at a Lloyd's syndicate or a flag state regulator and make the case that dark vessel detection is not a nice-to-have, it is the thing that keeps your marine insurance from getting repriced. They are too small for you to ignore and too far along for you to dismiss. If they were headquartered in Oslo instead of Luxembourg you would have already applied.

watch_list.why: Nonprofit flying two satellites for methane and CO2 point-source detection. Data partnerships and open data model directly parallel to GFW. Planet Labs analog but for emissions.
watch_list.roast: Carbon Mapper is the nonprofit that does what GHGSat charges a quarter million dollars per year to access. Two satellites up, free data portal, peer-reviewed methods, and a mission to name and shame super-emitters until someone turns the valve. You built a commercial licensing program on top of an open-access fisheries platform, Carbon Mapper is doing the same thing for methane plumes coming off landfills in Kern County and gas fields in the Permian. The difference is you had to convince the board that selling data was not evil. They started there. They just announced a next-generation sensor in April and they have OGCI as a partner, which means the oil majors are funding the stick they are about to get beaten with. If you liked the GFW model but wished the emitters had shorter response times and the data was updated weekly instead of annually, this is it. You would spend the first six months explaining to Gulf Coast operators why a free platform drives more behavior change than a paid one, and the second six months building the commercial tier for insurers and banks who need an API and an SLA. The work is harder than it looks because methane detection has more false positives than AIS and the attribution fights are vicious. But you have done vicious attribution fights. You did them with ENI and NOPSEMA for four years. You are good at them. Tromso to California, ice to methane, same skill set, worse air quality.

watch_list.why: AI platform for climate risk reporting and disaster response automation. Insurance, corporate buyers. Fast-growing adaptation sector (up 64% in 2025). Fits EO + risk + enterprise motion.
watch_list.roast: Beehive automates climate risk reporting and helps companies prepare for floods, fires, and the other disasters that used to be edge cases and are now quarterly events. They are riding the adaptation wave, which grew 64 percent to $5.5 billion in 2025 because everyone finally admitted that stopping climate change and surviving climate change are two different budget lines. The customer base is insurance, real estate, agriculture, and corporate risk teams who need to file climate risk disclosures and have no idea what data to use or how to explain why their Tampa office is underwater. You have spent a career translating satellite data into arguments that get people to spend money before the disaster happens, not after. Beehive is doing that but with a UI and an automated reporting layer so the risk manager does not have to learn what synthetic aperture radar is. This is not a data licensing play. It is a SaaS play built on top of someone else's EO data, which means the margin is better and the sales cycle is shorter, but you do not control the dataset and you are not the platform. You are building on top of the platform. That might feel like a step sideways after GFW and KSAT, or it might feel like relief. The pitch is easier. The value prop is obvious. The buyer has budget and mandate. Climate adaptation is the only climate tech segment where the tailwinds are product-market fit plus legislation plus weather. You would not be explaining why this matters. You would be explaining why Beehive does it better than the other six platforms the prospect is evaluating. That is a different kind of hard. You are good at different kinds of hard. You are less good at pretending SaaS sales cycles are interesting.

watch_list.why: Industrial heat is a fifth of global energy demand and almost nobody is decarbonizing it. Antora stores electricity as heat in carbon blocks, discharges as process heat or power. Real hardware, real customers, thermal storage finally crossing the demo chasm.
watch_list.roast: Antora builds thermal batteries that store electricity as heat in carbon blocks and you will spend three pitch meetings explaining why industrial process heat matters before the VP nods and says yeah okay that is actually huge. You sold satellite data to offshore oil majors who cared about visual confirmation of what their helicopters already saw. Antora is selling heat nobody can see to cement plants that have bought the same fuel oil for sixty years. The tech works. The market is a fifth of global energy demand. The conversations are going to feel like ENI in 2012 when you had to explain why proactive monitoring was better than waiting for a call from Greenpeace. Antora has $50 million from Lowercarbon and Breakthrough and they are building a commercial pilot in Fresno. They need someone who knows how to turn a pilot into a precedent and a precedent into an industry expectation. You know that pattern better than most of their team. The industrial heat category is boring, enormous, and five years from breaking open. You should be in the room when it does.

watch_list.why: Cement is 8% of global emissions. Sublime uses electrochemistry instead of kilns, has Microsoft offtake signed, and is building real production in Massachusetts. Paul's commercial toolkit is built for this: find the buyer exposed to regulatory or reputational pressure, structure a pilot that creates precedent, let the competitor fear drive the rest of the market.
watch_list.roast: Sublime Systems makes low carbon cement using electrochemistry instead of blasting limestone in a kiln at 1400 degrees and cement is eight percent of global emissions which means it is both boring and apocalyptically important. You will spend the first two months explaining to people that cement is not concrete and the next six months explaining why a construction buyer in Boston would pay more for a product that looks identical and performs the same but has a lower carbon intensity. The answer is regulation and reputation and corporate sustainability commitments and the fact that Microsoft already signed an offtake agreement which means this is not science fair anymore. Sublime has $75 million from Energy Impact Partners and they are building a commercial production facility in Massachusetts. They need someone who knows how to sell an identical product that costs more by finding the customer who cannot afford the reputational risk of not buying it. That was the Shell story, the Exxon story, the ENI story. It is the same pattern in a different hard hat. Cement is not sexy but it is everywhere and if Sublime makes this work every concrete supplier in North America will need to respond. You should be in the room when the dominoes start falling.

watch_list.why: Peer-reviewed satellite and AI MRV platform for farm, field, and forest carbon. Third-party validated. Independent. Growing fast in ag carbon markets.
watch_list.roast: So you built a satellite MRV platform for carbon. Congratulations, you and fifteen other companies discovered the same obvious application at the same time. Peer reviewed, you say. Third party validated. Independent. All the right words in the right order, like someone ran compliance Scrabble and decided that was a business model. Here is what I want to know. Who is actually paying you to measure farm carbon that the farmer did not already know was there, and why are they paying you instead of just looking at the USDA soil maps and calling it done? Because if the answer is voluntary carbon buyers hedging regulatory risk, then you are not building a carbon company, you are building a very expensive insurance product that stops existing the day Brussels or Sacramento writes the rule with enough teeth that someone has to actually care. I spent a decade at KSAT selling satellite monitoring to oil companies who did not want to buy it until a regulator made them, and another three years at GFW trying to sell derived satellite products to people who could already get the underlying imagery for free. The business model only works if you solve a problem someone will pay to solve before the rule forces them to solve it. So tell me this. What is the ENI story? What is the client who signs not because they have to but because their competitor just did and now they look exposed? Because that is the only commercial story that matters in satellite based environmental monitoring. You have good science. You probably have decent imagery partnerships. You maybe even have a product that works. But I have seen twenty versions of this pitch, and the ones that survive are the ones that figure out who has budget, who has urgency, and who will write a check this quarter instead of next year. If you can answer that, I will read the deck. If you cannot, you are going to spend two years wondering why everyone loves the demo and nobody closes.

watch_list.why: AI platform for natural disaster prep and response. Automates climate risk reporting. Trellis 2026 finalist. Raised funding, has traction with enterprise.
watch_list.roast: Beehive is an AI platform that automates climate risk reporting and helps companies prepare for and respond to natural disasters, which means you would spend half your time explaining to CFOs why climate risk is now a disclosure requirement and the other half explaining to operations teams why their existing disaster response plan is a binder that no one has opened since 2019. This is a compliance and resilience sale wrapped in climate language, and the buyer is someone who got surprised by a flood, a fire, or a regulatory letter and is now looking for a system that makes the problem go away. You know this buyer. You sold to them at GFW when you closed the insurance vertical with AXA Climate and British Marine. The pitch is the same. The risk is real, the cost of ignorance is climbing, and the tools that existed five years ago are not good enough anymore. Beehive is a Trellis Climate Tech 2026 finalist, which means they have traction and they have a story that investors believe. The question is whether they have figured out how to sell into enterprise procurement without getting stuck in six-month pilot hell. If they have a repeatable sales process and a customer list that includes at least two names you recognize, this is worth a conversation. If they are still figuring out who the buyer is, you could be the one who figures it out for them, but that means you are building the commercial function from scratch again, and you should ask yourself if that is the job you want or if you would rather join a place that already has pipeline and just needs someone to close it.

watch_list.why: Hyperspectral imaging from space. Unique dataset, hard to replicate. EO industry exec mentioned them in Payload 2026 State of EO. Small team, early traction.
watch_list.roast: Hyperspectral from space. Fantastic. You are flying a sensor that costs twice as much, produces ten times the data, and solves a problem that exactly four people on Earth actually understand well enough to pay for. Let me be very clear. I love hyperspectral. It is legitimately differentiated. It is technically hard. It is a dataset that nobody else has at the resolution and revisit you are promising. It is also commercially cursed, and you know it, because every hyperspectral company for the last fifteen years has followed the same pattern. Raise money on the science. Build the instrument. Launch the satellite. Spend two years explaining to potential customers what a hyperspectral signature is and why they should care. Burn the runway. Pivot to selling the data to three letter agencies. Get acquired for the tech. Here is why. Hyperspectral works when you know exactly what you are looking for and you need to see it in a scene full of noise. Methane from industrial background. Algae type in mixed coastal water. Mineral composition in regolith. Crop stress in a field that looks fine in RGB. All real. All valuable. All commercially hard because the buyer has to know they need hyperspectral before they know they need you, and most of them do not. They buy RGB or SAR because they understand it, because the Use Case is proven, and because twenty other vendors can bid the contract. You are not selling a capability. You are selling a market education program with a satellite attached. That is a tough business. So here is the question. Who is the customer who already gets it? Who has the budget, the use case, and the operational need, and who is buying this quarter not next year? Because if the answer is a mining major who needs lithium brine discrimination or an oil company who needs to prove they are not flaring, then you have something. If the answer is everyone will want this once they see the data, you are going to spend three years doing free pilots for companies that will never convert. I spent ten years selling EO services. The technology is never the constraint. The constraint is finding the buyer who knows what they do not know and has the authority to pay you to tell them. If you have that, call me. If you are still explaining the sensor to geologists, good luck.

watch_list.why: AI-driven satellite MRV for forest carbon. CEEZER integration. Direct Paul domain.
watch_list.roast: Kanop uses machine learning to interpret satellite imagery for forest carbon monitoring. Which means you would spend your time explaining to corporate buyers why their ML model sees what their consultant on the ground does not, which is exactly the kind of asymmetric-information arbitrage you lived at GFW. They just integrated with CEEZER, the carbon marketplace aggregator, so there is a live commercial channel with actual credit issuance volume behind it. The voluntary carbon market has more scar tissue than a telemark knee, but the MRV infrastructure play is different. You are not selling credits, you are selling the verification layer that makes credits credible, and that puts you one step closer to the regulator than the project developer. The company is young enough that you would be building the GTM motion, not inheriting someone else's debris field. Kanop has the satellite remote sensing skill set you know, the carbon market context you have lived, and the MRV credibility problem you spent five years at GFW trying to solve. If they have hired a VP of sales already, you are too late. If they have not, you are exactly on time.

watch_list.why: Hyperspectral imaging for methane, agriculture, mining. Mentioned in Payload EO 2026 state-of-industry. Edmonton-based.
watch_list.roast: Wyvern is building hyperspectral satellites out of Edmonton, which means they are smart enough to avoid the Bay Area wage structure and dumb enough to think Canadian government procurement will sustain them. Hyperspectral is the EO segment where everyone has been waiting for the commercial use case to show up for a decade, and Wyvern is betting that methane detection, precision agriculture, and mining monitoring will finally be it. They might be right. The EU Methane Regulation is real, the mining companies are tired of getting caught by NGO overhead passes, and precision ag has actual revenue if you can get past the integrator layer. You would be selling a data product that most buyers do not yet know they need, which is either the best job in EO or the worst, depending on whether you like playing the long game. Their CEO was quoted in the Payload EO industry roundup alongside ICEYE, BlackSky, and Planet, so they have gotten themselves into the conversation. But hyperspectral is still the sector where the physics is gorgeous and the revenue model is theoretical. You know how to build the pipeline when the market does not exist yet. The question is whether Wyvern has the balance sheet to let you run that play for eighteen months.

watch_list.why: MRV platform for enhanced rock weathering carbon removal. US-based. Emerging CDR vertical.
watch_list.roast: CREW Carbon is building the MRV stack for enhanced rock weathering, which is the carbon removal method where you grind up rocks, spread them on fields, and wait for chemistry to happen. It is slower than biochar, less sexy than DAC, and harder to verify than trees, which makes it exactly the kind of market where an MRV platform could actually matter. The science is sound, the permanence is real, and the monitoring problem is genuinely hard, because you are trying to measure a geochemical process happening in soil over years, not counting trees from space. If they have solved that, they have a wedge. The carbon removal market is bifurcating into the technologies that have pilot-scale buyers now and the technologies that will have compliance-scale buyers in three years. Enhanced rock weathering is in the second bucket, which means you would be selling to corporates who are building future portfolios, not firefighting today's Scope 3 reporting. That is either visionary or early, and the difference is whether the buyers show up in 2027 or 2029. You have done the market development timing bet twice, and you were right both times. CREW Carbon would be the third.

watch_list.why: Generates water from atmosphere using waste heat from AI data centers. Climate adaptation meets AI infrastructure play. Trellis Climate Tech 2026 finalist.
watch_list.roast: You spent a decade selling satellite data to people who did not want to admit they needed it, and now WAVR is selling atmospheric water extraction to hyperscalers who do not want to admit their data centers are cooking the planet while draining aquifers. The pitch is gorgeous in its irony. AI needs electricity, electricity needs cooling, cooling needs water, and water is the thing the West does not have. So WAVR pulls it out of the air using the waste heat from the very compute that is accelerating the water crisis. It is a business model that only makes sense because we have made such a spectacular mess of things. You will spend the first three months of any conversation explaining why this is not a dehumidifier, and the second three months explaining why the unit economics actually work at scale. The good news is that every KSAT pitch you made to a skeptical oil major in Stavanger or Perth was training for this. You know how to sell a technology people do not understand to buyers who are not sure they believe the problem is real yet. The better news is that the problem is extremely real, the buyer pool is growing fast, and WAVR has actual traction. If they have already closed pilots with any of the big four hyperscalers, you should be on a plane tomorrow. If they have not, you should be the one who gets them there. Either way, the category is going to explode, and getting in early means you get to define what the commercial function looks like instead of inheriting someone else's mess.

watch_list.why: Converts industrial waste into useful chemicals via electrochemistry. Deep industrial sales, complex value chains, same buyer skepticism as early KSAT oil and gas.
watch_list.roast: Aepnus has built an electrochemical process that turns industrial waste streams into commodity chemicals, which sounds like alchemy until you realize it is just really good engineering and a willingness to do the ugly work that venture-backed software founders will not touch. This is the kind of company that will make you spend a month on-site at a chemical plant in Louisiana learning why the plant manager does not trust anything that did not exist in 1987, and then another month building a pilot proposal that has to clear procurement, safety, environmental, operations, and corporate before you get a maybe. It is a grind. The sale cycle is 18 months if you are lucky, two years if you are not, and the first three deals will teach you more about industrial chemistry than you ever wanted to know. But here is the thing. You have done this. You sold satellite monitoring to Exxon when they did not think they needed it. You convinced Shell to switch vendors after a decade of MDA. You know how to find the one person inside a massive organization who actually has budget authority and pain, and you know how to build a business case that survives four layers of internal review. Aepnus needs someone who can do exactly that, and they need it now, because the market is starting to move and the companies that figure out commercial traction in the next 18 months are going to own the category. If they have already signed a paid pilot with a Fortune 500 industrial, you should take the call. If they have not, you should be the person who closes it. The category is real, the technology works, and the team is serious. The question is whether they can build a commercial engine before they run out of runway. That is a question you know how to answer.

watch_list.why: Farmer-led carbon removal credits with AI and satellite MRV. Combines EO, carbon markets, and commercial structuring. Fits GFW Planet deal pattern and ENI ecosystem-building skill.
watch_list.roast: Earthbanc is financing reforestation and agroforestry projects in the Global South, then selling the carbon credits to corporate buyers who need offsets that will survive scrutiny. The MRV stack uses satellite remote sensing, AI, and a farmer-facing app, which means you would be selling both up the value chain to carbon buyers and down the value chain to the farmers and project developers who need financing. It is a two-sided marketplace problem dressed up as a climate solution, and the commercial model is held together by the belief that someone will pay a premium for credits that are actually additional, permanent, and verifiable. You have seen this movie before. At GFW, you built a commercial licensing program on top of a platform that was designed to give data away for free, and you had to convince buyers that paying for a relationship was worth it even when the core product was open access. Earthbanc has the same tension. There are free satellite tools everywhere, so why pay Earthbanc? The answer is the same as it was at GFW. The buyers who pay are not paying because the free version is insufficient. They are paying because their use case requires a structured commercial relationship, a verified methodology, and a partner willing to stand behind the outputs. If Earthbanc has already signed corporate buyers and has a pipeline of farmer projects that are actually being monitored, this is a real business. If they are still pre-revenue and looking for someone to build the commercial function from scratch, that is also interesting, but it means you are going to spend the first year doing exactly what you did at GFW, which is building the internal permission to sell before you can build the external pipeline. Either way, the domain is directly adjacent to everything you know, and the skills are a perfect match. The question is whether the team has the patience and the capital to let you build it right.

watch_list.why: Advanced chemical filtration DAC with basalt mineralization partnership. Emerging player in permanent carbon removal.
watch_list.roast: You spent ten years watching Shell and ExxonMobil tell themselves they would buy carbon offsets someday when the price was right and the board stopped asking questions about quarterly returns. Sirona is building DAC machines with the same promise every other DAC company makes, which is that the cost curve will improve and the corporate buyers will appear and the mineralization in basalt will be permanent and auditable. The difference is steam regeneration instead of heat and a partnership model with injection providers instead of vertically integrated infrastructure. That modularity matters in a market where nobody can afford to build end to end and the buyers who show up want proof of sequestration not a science project. The ENI pattern holds. The company that can structure a pilot to set a regulatory precedent or create a corporate reporting use case that makes the status quo riskier than the experiment wins the long game. Sirona has the partnership model. Whether they have the commercial instinct to go find the buyer with the most to lose and turn a pilot into a standard is the whole question. That is not a technology problem. It is a Paul problem.

watch_list.why: Seven-figure and eight-figure international contracts, sovereign satellite sales plus commercial tasking. Direct GFW/KSAT domain overlap.
watch_list.roast: Vantor announced three contracts worth a combined 204.7 million dollars with Middle East and Africa customers and a multi-million-dollar deal with a South Asian government in the span of two months and nobody outside the defense trade press noticed. That is either the best or the worst sign depending on whether you are trying to win commercial market share or build a sustainable government services business that does not depend on Beltway RFPs. The model is elegant. Sell a country one or two satellites so they have sovereign infrastructure and then sell them ten years of commercial tasking and imagery because two satellites cannot provide the refresh rate or revisit cadence they actually need. The gap between owning the infrastructure and operating the mission is where the revenue lives. You sold this gap at KSAT for a decade. Shell bought the MLA because they could not run the operation themselves. ENI signed because they needed someone who knew how to move NOPSEMA. The pitch writes itself. The question is whether Vantor wants someone who can sell the service or someone who can run the program and whether they understand that the former becomes the latter if you hire correctly. Also whether you are prepared to explain to your Taos friends that you are back in the business of selling satellite imagery to governments that do things with it you would rather not think about too hard. That part does not get easier.

watch_list.why: Full-stack biochar platform, agribusiness deployment model, emerging markets focus. YC-backed. Practical carbon removal at scale.
watch_list.roast: Terraton is a San Francisco biochar company founded by two people who used to work at Google and they are deploying biochar facilities in emerging markets and generating carbon credits and the entire pitch is we will make carbon removal a product not a science experiment. Charm Industrial is doing the same thing with biomass liquids. Varaha is doing it in India with cost discipline and verified credits. CarbonZero is doing it with 16-year-old founders and thirty thousand ton kilns. Everyone in the biochar space is racing to prove the unit economics work before the voluntary carbon market figures out which removal pathways are real and which ones are accounting. The companies that survive are the ones that have corporate offtake agreements and third-party verification and a path to profit before the next market correction. Terraton has the full-stack model and the agribusiness partnerships and the YC brand which gets you meetings but does not close deals. What they need is someone who has spent fifteen years explaining to CFOs why paying for something invisible today prevents a much larger visible cost in three years. Someone who knows how to structure a pilot so the customer feels like they are setting a standard not taking a risk. Someone who understands that the sale is not the biochar it is the compliance narrative and the stakeholder choreography and the regulatory air cover. You have done this in three sectors and two continents. The question is whether you want to do it again for a technology that might actually matter or whether you are tired of being the person in the room who has to explain why the thing that makes sense is also the thing nobody wants to be first to buy.

watch_list.why: French government multi-year contract for 10-satellite constellation, multi-sensor architecture, sovereign EO capability. Paul already applied but worth a roast.
watch_list.roast: Wait, you already sent them a resume? Of course you did. Because when a company announces a multi-year French government contract worth tens of millions of euros to deploy 10 satellites with optical, thermal infrared, hyperspectral, and RF sensors all on one bus, your Pavlovian response is to write a cover letter. Loft is doing the thing you spent a decade at KSAT trying to explain to oil majors: you can put more than one sensor on a satellite and get answers instead of just pictures. They are calling it AI-enabled space infrastructure. You are calling it Tuesday. The program is sovereign, which means the sales cycle is over before it started, but the operational build-out is just beginning. If they had hired you in March, you would be in Paris right now explaining to CNES why the fourth satellite needs a different orbit. Instead, you are in Taos refreshing this dashboard. First launch is Q4 2026. By then you will be somewhere else, watching their press release, wondering if they even opened your email.

watch_list.why: Seven GRUS-3 satellites launching 2026, 2.2m resolution, existing constellation serving 30+ countries, AxelGlobe commercial platform strong fit for Paul's EO data licensing background.
watch_list.roast: Japanese microsatellite company launching seven GRUS-3 satellites in 2026 to expand their constellation past ten birds. Each one is 150kg, 2.2-meter resolution, seven bands including coastal blue and red edge, which is more color options than you have had in your dating life since the divorce. Axelspace has been at this since 2008, which makes them older than New Space but younger than your MBA. They serve government and commercial customers in more than 30 countries, which sounds impressive until you realize that KSAT served more than that by 2015 and you were the one who built half the deck. Their AxelGlobe platform sells Earth observation data for precision agriculture, forest monitoring, and financial product development, which is exactly the commercial licensing model you built at GFW except theirs makes money and yours got defunded. The company is in Tokyo. You are in Taos. The timezone makes Tromso look convenient. But they have the data flow, the manufacturing cadence, and the customer base. You know how to sell this product because you have sold worse versions of it to harder buyers. The question is whether they know they need you, or whether they think the satellites do the work themselves.

morning_briefs.notes: Daily / Morning Brief cron commit landed at 12:30 UTC but no morning_briefs entry was appended to dashboard-data.json (existing pipeline bug, last appended entry was 2026-05-01). This run filled the gap. Digest had 276 postings of which ~225 were aggregator-board URLs (Terra.do, Climatebase, Climate Change Jobs by city, Schmidt Marine listings, Esri portfolio, etc.) that link to listing pages rather than specific JDs and are unscoreable per rubric. After applying applications-dedup, watch_list checks, hard exclusions, title-level filtering for engineering/analyst/IC-SDR/recruiter roles, and the recently-ranked window, 8 specific JDs were dispatched to scoring subagents. Subagents could not fetch JDs from their sandboxed environment (WebFetch denied), so fetching was retried in main context. Sierra Club returned 403 (unreadable). 5 successful fetches plus 2 hard-exclusion title-checks plus 1 already-known-pattern (Bentley) produced 4 new Tier 3 dashboard entries: Bees & Bears Head of Risk Berlin (44.5), Bentley Principal Consultant Remote/GA (47.5), SF Estuary Institute Executive Director Richmond CA (52.0), Active Site Chief of Staff Cambridge MA (43.5). No Tier 1 or Tier 2 entries today, no deep dives triggered.

haiku:
Sox face Rays at four
nine wins against thirteen
Pats play Seattle

Mother's Day weekend
whitewater roars through the gorge
boaters grip the line

Victory Day shrunk
Red Square sealed by decree
Zelenskyy permits

ICEYE rides share up
Lynk deploys tower seven
Exolaunch delivers

Microplastics warm
regionally exceed carbon
we breathe and we heat

Patriots schedule
released next week, Munich game
Seahawks open week

Hormuz threat aborts
thirty-six hours, then silence
the wire stays cold

Sox host Tampa one
thirty-five, Fenway in May
Wheeler still holds snow

Super El Niño
climate models all agree
monsoon holds its cards

NASA rebrands death
Falcon and Eagle rise up
White House lost that round

Merlin by October
daily one meter coverage
Planet sweats the math

Great Barrier bleached out
worst summer on record now
coral capitalism

Red Sox nine and thirteen
April mud turned into May
the dashboard refreshes

Redhead asks the plan
after the wire goes quiet
satellite silence

Trump walked back the ships
Operation Freedom died
still no ranked inbox

NASA names new birds
after raptors, not acronyms
marketing learns fast

Hormuz ten weeks shut
Trump rejects Iran's reply
oil over one hundred

Strait closed, pumps rising
fed gas tax suspended soon
Saudi warns all year

Red River trout stocked
Eagle Rock Lake fat and full
Questa kids did that

Mexican wolf crossed
radio collar southbound
first time in decades

Prescribed fires out
Carson managers declare
fall smoke finally done

Microplastics warm
darker bits absorb the heat
Nature Climate says

Antarctic spiral
triple whammy downward pull
ocean turns from friend

Colorado snow
smallest pack on record
Lake Powell runs dry

Kansas wheat dying
forty four percent gone poor
heat took the whole crop

Psyche flyby Mars
four thousand five hundred k's
May fifteenth at speed

Artemis splashed down
Integrity in the sea
April eleventh won

Mother's Day Races
whitewater May eight through ten
sixty eighth year running

Spring in Taos quiet
sage blooms, gorge holds light all day
the wire is clean now

Iran ceasefire
on massive life support, Trump says
gas hits four fifty

Mexican gray wolf
crossed south for first time in years
Carson fires all out

Sox host Phillies
six forty-five at Fenway
Rafaela still bunts

Marty Makary out
FDA chief resigns today
mass layoffs, no trust

War costs twenty-nine
billion so far, Pentagon says
I don't think about you

Strait of Hormuz closed
ADNOC at sixty percent
Shell profits on war

Nebraska primaries
West Virginia votes today
blue dot holds the line

ICEYE Vantor both
going public this year, they say
launch costs still squeeze hard

Sox lost to Phils two-one
worst home record in AL
Fenway's silent bats

EPA repealed
the endangerment finding
science now optional

Mexican wolf crossed
into Mexico for the first
first time in decades

Open Cosmos built
eight new sats for disaster
Europe's eye awake

Gorge bridge gets tall rails
eight million dollar deterrent
six to eight feet high

Redhead at the bar
telemark stance while she laughs
I should say something

Resume sent again
tracker scrapes two-thirty jobs
